title: Logarithms and the laws of logs

screens.prompt: The lab colony from last lesson doubles every hour. How many hours does it take to become exactly 8 times its starting size?
screens.reveal: Right, 3 hours: $2^3 = 8$. The question "how many times do I multiply by 2 to reach 8?" is exactly what a **logarithm** answers. A logarithm is the inverse of an exponential, and this lesson makes that idea precise and general.

screens.prompt: $\log_2(8) = 3$ means the same thing as which statement?

choices.feedback: This swaps the base and the exponent. The log's base (2) becomes the power's base, and the log's value (3) becomes the exponent: $2^3=8$, not $3^2$.
This puts the argument of the log (8) in the wrong place. $\log_2(8)=3$ unpacks to base$^{\text{value}}=$argument, i.e. $2^3=8$.

screens.successFeedback: Right. In general, $\log_a(x) = y$ means exactly $a^y = x$: the log's base becomes the power's base, and the log's value becomes the exponent.

screens.prompt: Evaluate $\log_2(32)$ without a calculator. (Write 32 as a power of 2 first.)

Because $e^x$ is its own derivative, the tangent gradient at any point equals the curve's height $e^x$ there. So the point where the gradient reads 5 is exactly the point where $e^x=5$, which is $x=\ln 5$. Drag along $y=e^x$ until the tangent gradient reads about 5.

goal.description: Target: tangent gradient ≈ 5, i.e. e^x ≈ 5, i.e. x ≈ ln 5.

screens.successFeedback: That's $x \approx 1.61$, which is exactly $\ln 5$. You just located a logarithm by dragging, not by pressing a log button, that's the geometric meaning of $\ln 5$: the point where $e^x$ (and its own gradient) equals 5.
hints: Move the point to the right, past $x=1$, where $e^x$ climbs above 4.
You're looking for somewhere between $x=1.5$ and $x=1.7$.
At $x \approx 1.61$, $e^x \approx 5$, so the gradient there is also $\approx 5$.

screens.body:
The laws of logs follow from the laws of indices. Simplify $2\ln3 + \ln5 - \ln15$ into a single logarithm:

$$
2\ln3 + \ln5 - \ln15 = \ln(3^2) + \ln5 - \ln15 = \ln(9\times5) - \ln15 = \ln45 - \ln15 = \ln\!\left(\frac{45}{15}\right) = \ln3.
$$
screens.selfExplainPrompt: Which law turned $2\ln3$ into $\ln9$, and which law then combined $\ln9+\ln5$ into $\ln45$?
screens.selfExplainAnswer: The power law, $n\log_a A = \log_a(A^n)$, turns $2\ln3$ into $\ln(3^2)=\ln9$. Then the product law, $\log_a A+\log_a B=\log_a(AB)$, combines $\ln9+\ln5$ into $\ln(9\times5)=\ln45$. Finally the quotient law, $\log_a A-\log_a B=\log_a(A/B)$, turns $\ln45-\ln15$ into $\ln(45/15)=\ln3$.

screens.worked: Using the power law then the product law: $3\ln2 + \ln5 = \ln(2^3) + \ln5 = \ln8 + \ln5 = \ln40$.
screens.prompt: Using the power law then the quotient law, simplify $2\ln4 - \ln2$ to $\ln(\text{some whole number})$. Give that whole number.
screens.successFeedback: Right: $2\ln4 = \ln(4^2) = \ln16$, and $\ln16 - \ln2 = \ln(16/2) = \ln8$. So the whole number is 8.

screens.prompt: You predicted the colony takes 3 hours to reach 8 times its size, since $2^3=8$. Calculators usually only have $\log_{10}$ and $\ln$ buttons, so to evaluate a log in another base, use the change of base formula $\log_a(x) = \dfrac{\ln x}{\ln a}$. Use it to evaluate $\log_5(20)$, to 3 decimal places.
screens.successFeedback: Right, $\log_5(20) = \dfrac{\ln20}{\ln5} \approx \dfrac{2.9957}{1.6094} \approx 1.861$. Counting doublings and reading logs off a graph both give the exact same kind of answer; change of base just lets any calculator reach it. Next lesson puts logs to work solving equations you can't just count your way to, plus growth and decay models.
